title: Family Consequences of Children's Disabilities By Dennis Hogan Russell Sage Foundation. 2012. 132 pages. $27.50 paper
abstract: This book is part of the American Sociological Association's Rose Series and purports to “draw an original, realistic, and comprehensive picture of families raising children with disabilities.” The Rose Series editors are to be commended for including a book on this important, yet often overlooked, topic. The author is a Distinguished Professor of Population Studies and Professor of Sociology at Brown University. The volume is unique among studies of families with disabled children in combining population-based data from several large surveys with qualitative data from observation and semi-structured interviews with a small number of families. According to the author, the book's primary intended audiences are parents of children with disabilities and professionals who work with such families. The book includes chapters on family dissolution, family life, the transition to adulthood, the experience of siblings, and access to services, among other topics. These are all important areas of research and reflect the topics that have been widely covered in the literature in this area. The qualitative findings are presented primarily through quotes from parents, and the quantitative results are clearly illustrated in graphs.
